retrieved_docs.title: Hypocalcemia -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: QTc prolongation: This can lead to torsades de pointe, a ventricular tachycardia that can be fatal.

retrieved_docs.title: Asystole -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Other electrolyte derangements: hypomagnesemia, hypocalcemia

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperkalemia -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac arrest Weakness Arrhythmias Paralysis

retrieved_docs.title: Hypocalcemia -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with severe hypocalcemia of less than 7 mg/dl and those with an acute drop in calcium level can develop seizures or life-threatening arrhythmias. Checking an ECG and aggressively correcting calcium levels in these situations is imperative. Patients with poorly controlled hypoparathyroidism are at increased risk of developing kidney problems, including kidney stones and chronic kidney disease, and overall decreased quality of life. [36] [37]

retrieved_docs.title: Hypermagnesemia -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with symptomatic hypermagnesemia can present different clinical manifestations depending on the level and the time in which the electrolytic disturbance has occurred. Hypermagnesemia is generally well tolerated. Thus patients with altered values (under 4 mg/dL) may be asymptomatic or paucisymptomatic. The most frequent symptoms and signs may include weakness, nausea, dizziness, and confusion (less than 7.0 mg/dL). Increasing values (7 to 12 mg/dL) induce decreased reflexes, worsening confusional state, drowsiness, bladder paralysis, flushing, headache, and constipation. A slight reduction in blood pressure and blurred vision caused by diminished accommodation and convergence can manifest. For higher values (over 12.0 mg/dL) muscle paralysis, paralytic ileus, decreased breathing rate, low blood pressure, electrocardiogram (ECG) changes including an increase in PR and QRS interval with sinus bradycardia, and atrioventricular block, coma and cardiac arrest (exceeding 15.0 mg/dL) may occur. When associated with hypocalcemia, hypermagnesemia may induce choreiform movements and seizures. The clinical picture becomes particularly severe, and there are few case reports of patients who survived to higher hypermagnesaemia levels. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Resistant Hypercalcemia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: EKG features of hypercalcemia often include PR interval and QRS prolongation, ST elevation, T wave inversion, and a J wave. It is crucial to perform imaging studies to evaluate for possible malignancies. Surgical exploration of the neck should be performed in all resistant hypercalcemia cases, especially when other localizing investigations have proven futile and the etiology remains uncertain.

retrieved_docs.title: Resistant Hypercalcemia -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Complications of Hypercalcemia can be related to the disease process itself or due to the side effects of therapies. Severely elevated hypercalcemia is an endocrine emergency and requires prompt management to avoid serious complications, which include severe volume contraction and cardiac and neurological consequences. [34] Complications from untreated hypercalcemia include the induction of abnormal heart rhythms. The electrocardiogram often shows T-wave flattening, mild prolongation of QRS and PR intervals, and the presence of Q-wave.

retrieved_docs.title: Chvostek Sign -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Among the most common postoperative complications following thyroid surgery is hypocalcemia. Research by Rio Del et al. showed a 13.8% incidence of hypocalcemia status post a thyroid lobectomy and a 38.8% incidence status post total thyroidectomy. [5] QT prolongation is a severe complication of hypocalcemia because it can precipitate deadly arrhythmias. [8] Ventricular arrhythmias are among the most critical and can lead to cardiac arrest. Supplementation with calcium carbonate in a patient who has undergone thyroid surgery has become common practice because of the potential for hypocalcemia. [9] Patients receiving calcium supplementation should avoid proton pump inhibitors because they are well known to cause worsening of hypocalcemia. [10] As discussed above, there is a minimal correlation between calcium levels and the Chvostek sign; although this may be true, if tetany presents with stimulation of the facial nerve, it may be beneficial to check the serum calcium level.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Parathyroid Hormone -- Clinical Significance -- Calcium's Role
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium is a divalent cation essential to heart, kidney, bone, and nervous system functioning, making PTH's functioning crucial. Calcium plays an integral part in cardiac contractions. The contractility of the heart is predicated on the availability and role of calcium inside myocardial cells. When there is an excess amount of calcium within cardiac cells, contractility will increase, and similarly, when there is a lesser concentration of calcium within the cardiac cells, contraction will decrease. This can potentially lead to prolonged QT intervals seen on ECG. Extreme hypercalcemia's effect on the myocardium can be manifested in ECG changes, causing very short QT intervals. This could potentially precipitate the onset of fatal arrhythmias such as ventricular tachycardia or even ventricular fibrillation if gone unattended. Abnormal oversecretion of PTH is also the source of bone degradation, systemically releasing dangerous amounts of calcium into the blood. This can facilitate the premature transition into osteoporosis and increase the susceptibility to fractures. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Hypocalcemia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Confirmation of hypocalcemia: The first part of the evaluation should focus on confirming the hypocalcemia by checking a serum albumin level to correct the total calcium or directly measuring the ionized calcium level. For each 1 g/dL decrease in albumin below 4 g/dl, 0.8 mg/dL should be added to the total serum calcium level. Checking cardiac stability: An ECG should be obtained to look for QTc prolongation, which, if present, is a risk factor for torsades de pointes. This is often treated with IV magnesium. [31] Determining the etiology of hypocalcemia: Once hypocalcemia is confirmed, further testing should be done to determine the etiology. This entails checking other electrolytes, such as serum magnesium and phosphorus levels, intact PTH, and vitamin D levels. Some causes are apparent, as in patients post-thyroidectomy or parathyroidectomy. Other biomarkers may be obtained as indicated by history and physical exam (eg, serum lipase in suspected pancreatitis.)

retrieved_docs.title: QT Prolonging Drugs -- Indications -- Prolonged QT Etiologies
retrieved_docs.content: Pharmacological Long QT syndrome Jervell and Lange-Neilson syndromes [9] Romano-Ward syndrome Hypocalcemia Hypokalemia Hypomagnesemia Hypothyroidism Hypothermia

retrieved_docs.title: Hypercalcemia -- Evaluation -- Laboratory
retrieved_docs.content: ECG: Short QT interval, low amplitude T-wave, ST-segment elevation, PR prolongation, tall and wide QRS, and arrhythmias, including bradycardia and premature ventricular contractions. Renal ultrasound: Chronic hypercalcemia can lead to the formation of renal stones, which can be visualized on ultrasound. Bone density scan or dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA): This scan may reveal osteoporosis related to primary hyperparathyroidism.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypocalcemia -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Tetany: Generally induced by a rapid decline in serum ionized calcium. Tetany is usually more dangerous and most commonly seen in the presence of respiratory alkalosis, causing hypocalcemia.

retrieved_docs.title: Calcium Gluconate -- Mechanism of Action -- Hypocalcemia
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms include circumoral paresthesias, muscle cramps, myalgias, dysphagia, depression, confusion, irritability, seizures, tetany, laryngospasm, and hypotension. Physical examination findings include hyperreflexia, carpopedal spasm, and the Trousseau and Chvostek signs. On EKG, hypocalcemia presents with prolonged QT interval, but its significance is undetermined as it is rare for calcium derangements to be the cause of cardiac arrest. EKG abnormalities of hypocalcemia, such as QT prolongation, typically respond to IV calcium gluconate, returning the QT interval to baseline. [1] [2] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: BRASH Syndrome -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: An electrocardiogram (ECG) is crucial in differentiating BRASH syndrome from other causes of bradycardia, such as isolated hyperkalemia, hypothermia, or hypothyroidism (see Image. Electrocardiographic Findings in a Patient with BRASH Syndrome). However, these findings are commonly underwhelming as an ECG does not typically show the stereotypical electrocardiographic changes observed in patients with hyperkalemia. A progression of electrocardiographic changes occurs as serum potassium levels elevate, starting with peaked T-waves and PR interval prolongation, followed by QRS interval widening and subsequent bradycardia with a sinusoidal rhythm. [8] These morphologies are commonly absent in patients with BRASH syndrome.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 1614
retrieved_docs.content: Chlorid vápenatý (calcium chloride) is associated with serious cardiac complications, including توقف القلب (cardiac arrest), and its use is contraindicated in the presence of فرط كالسيوم الدم (hyper‑calcemia). Recognizing that calcium chloride can precipitate life‑threatening arrhythmias and that hyper‑calcemia represents a condition in which calcium chloride should not be administered is essential for safe patient management and for preventing adverse outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1291
retrieved_docs.content: Chlorid vápenatý (calcium chloride) is identified as a contraindicated agent in several serious clinical situations. Its administration should be avoided in patients who are experiencing cardiac arrest and ventricular fibrillation, as the drug is considered contraindicated in the context of these life‑threatening arrhythmias. In addition, chlorid vápenatý is contraindicated in the presence of فرط كالسيوم الدم (hyper‑calcemia), because giving additional calcium could exacerbate the already elevated serum calcium levels. These contraindications underscore the importance of careful assessment of cardiac rhythm disturbances and calcium metabolism status before considering calcium chloride therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1926
retrieved_docs.content: Acidosis (الحماض) and hyperkalemia (فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم) are medical conditions that frequently co‑occur. In clinical practice, the presence of an acid‑base disturbance such as metabolic or respiratory acidosis is often associated with elevated serum potassium levels, and conversely, high potassium concentrations can exacerbate or signal underlying acidotic states. Recognizing this interrelationship is important for accurate diagnosis, monitoring, and therapeutic management of patients who present with either condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1663
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac arrest and ventricular fibrillation are acute life‑threatening cardiac events for which the administration of glukonát vápenatý (calcium gluconate) is contraindicated, indicating that this calcium salt should be avoided in these specific emergency situations. Conversely, glukonát vápenatý is a therapeutic agent used to correct نقص كالسيوم الدم (hypocalcemia), a condition characterized by low blood calcium levels. This dual relationship underscores the importance of recognizing both the appropriate indication of calcium gluconate for treating hypocalcemia and its contraindication in the context of cardiac arrest and ventricular fibrillation, thereby guiding safe and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1524
retrieved_docs.content: Chlorid vápenatý (calcium chloride) can be employed in the treatment of توقف القلب (cardiac arrest), but its administration is contraindicated in the presence of رَجَفانٌ بطيني (ventricular fibrillation) and in patients with فرط كالسيوم الدم (hypercalcemia). Recognizing these therapeutic indications and contraindications is essential for safe patient care and for avoiding adverse outcomes when using chlorid vápenatý.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2449
retrieved_docs.content: Sodium phosphate is classified among the drugs used for treatment of hypercalcemia, indicating its role in managing elevated calcium levels.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2345
retrieved_docs.content: Hyperkalemia (فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم) is clinically associated with low blood glucose (نقص تركيز السكر), and it frequently co‑occurs with acidosis (الحماض). This interrelationship indicates that patients who present with hyperkalemia should be evaluated for possible hypoglycemia and metabolic acidosis, as the simultaneous presence of these disturbances can affect diagnosis, treatment decisions, and overall patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2852
retrieved_docs.content: In clinical assessment, results that are above the reference range are classified as increased findings, and an increased finding is interpreted as indicating tachycardia (تسرع القلب).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 4 -- Community 2776
retrieved_docs.content: Chlorid vápenatý, a calcium chloride preparation, is contraindicated in patients with رَجَفانٌ بطيني (ventricular fibrillation) and in those who have فرط كالسيوم الدم (hyper‑calcemia), because administration of calcium chloride in these conditions can exacerbate cardiac arrhythmia or further increase already elevated serum calcium levels.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2282
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac arrest and ventricular fibrillation are clinical situations in which the use of digoxin is contraindicated, meaning that digoxin should not be administered to patients experiencing these life‑threatening cardiac arrhythmias.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1435
retrieved_docs.content: Parathormon, also known as parathyroid hormone, is classified as a member of the “parathyroid hormones and analogues” group. It is employed therapeutically to correct نقص كالسيوم الدم (blood calcium deficiency or hypocalcemia), reflecting its central role in raising serum calcium levels. Calcitonin is described as having a relationship with parathormon, highlighting the complementary and often opposing actions of these two hormones in the regulation of calcium metabolism. Understanding the classification of parathormon, its use in treating hypocalcemia, and its interaction with calcitonin is essential for managing disorders of calcium balance and for guiding appropriate patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 4 -- Community 2708
retrieved_docs.content: Alcohol withdrawal syndrome (متلازمة الامتناع عن تعاطي الكحول) is clinically associated with hypokalemia syndrome (متلازمة نقصُ البوتاسيوم في الدَّم). This association indicates that individuals undergoing alcohol withdrawal are at risk of developing significant potassium depletion, which can manifest as hypokalemia and its related complications. Recognizing this link is important for clinicians, who should monitor serum potassium levels in patients with alcohol withdrawal and provide appropriate electrolyte replacement to prevent cardiac arrhythmias, muscle weakness, and other adverse outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1820
retrieved_docs.content: Electrolyte imbalance (اختلال توازن الإلكتروليتات) is a disorder characterized by abnormal concentrations of key ions in the blood, and it encompasses several specific clinical entities. One component of this disorder is potassium deficiency syndrome (متلازمة نقصُ البوتاسيوم في الدَّم), which reflects low serum potassium levels and can lead to muscle weakness, cardiac arrhythmias, and metabolic disturbances. Another manifestation is an increased anion gap (زيادة الفجوة الأنيونية), indicating the presence of unmeasured anions such as lactate or ketoacids and often signaling metabolic acidosis. Hyperuricemia (فرط حمض يوريك الدم), the elevation of serum uric acid, may also arise within the context of electrolyte disturbances and is associated with gout, kidney stones, and renal impairment. Finally, low blood chloride (نقص كلوريد الدم) represents hypochloremia, a condition that can exacerbate metabolic alkalosis and affect fluid balance. Together, these conditions illustrate the diverse ways in which electrolyte homeostasis can be disrupted, highlighting the importance of comprehensive assessment and correction of electrolyte abnormalities in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 4 -- Community 2843
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac arrest and ventricular fibrillation are clinical situations in which the use of digitoxin is contraindicated; digitoxin should not be administered to patients experiencing these life‑threatening arrhythmias because it can exacerbate cardiac instability.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1378
retrieved_docs.content: Bretylium tosylate is an antiarrhythmic agent that may prevent the occurrence of cardiac arrest and ventricular fibrillation, thereby contributing to the management of life‑threatening cardiac rhythm disturbances.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2020
retrieved_docs.content: Potassium ion and potassium supplements are directly linked, as potassium supplements are formulations designed to provide potassium ion to the body. By delivering potassium ion, these supplements help correct potassium deficiencies, support normal electrolyte balance, and maintain proper cellular function, making them an important therapeutic option in the management of conditions such as hypokalemia.
